channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Oil Buyers Battered by the Iran War Energy Crisis Race to Build Buffers
item.description: As rising fuel costs clobber their economies, the Philippines and other developing countries in Asia are pushing to build strategic petroleum reserves to cushion against future shocks.
item.dc:creator: River Akira Davis
item.media:credit: Jes Aznar for The New York Times
item.media:description: A road in Manila. After the start of the war in Iran, gasoline prices doubled and diesel prices tripled in the Philippines, setting off a sudden spike in inflation and transit strikes.

item.title: Lebanese Army Accuses Israeli Troops of Firing as Its Forces Deploy in ‘Pilot Zone’
item.description: The Israeli military was withdrawing from a village under a U.S.-brokered deal to transfer control of occupied areas to Lebanon after months of war. The Israeli military did not immediately comment.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: Lebanese army troops in Zawtar al-Gharbiya, southern Lebanon, after Israeli forces withdrew from the area on Tuesday.

item.title: Pentagon Identifies Soldier Killed in Iran War as Sgt. Michael Emmanuel Swinton
item.description: Sgt. Michael Emmanuel Swinton, the 17th service member to be named, was killed during the disposal of a drone in northern Iraq.
item.dc:creator: Jacey Fortin, Pooja Salhotra and Maia Spoto
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Trump Escalates Canada Tariffs as Mark Carney Holds Firm
item.description: The president’s new tariffs on a top U.S. trading partner aim at gaining concessions, but the Canadian government, and the public, have been bracing for the escalation.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:credit: Nick Iwanyshyn/Reuters
item.media:description: Steel coils at the Algoma Steel facility in Ontario in July. U.S. tariffs on goods such as steel, aluminum and vehicles have hit sensitive Canadian sectors.

item.title: 1,700 H.I.V. Treatment Sites Closed After Trump Aid Cuts, a Study Finds
item.description: New research also shows that children and high-risk adult populations have been particularly affected.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: amfAR, The Foundation for AIDS Research
President's Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief
item.media:credit: Joao Silva/The New York Times
item.media:description: A health clinic in Johannesburg that was closed in 2025, following cuts in U.S. aid.

item.title: Cameron Hamilton Poised to Lead FEMA Once More
item.description: Cameron Hamilton defended the agency last year when President Trump talked about eliminating it. Emergency managers hope for more stability if he’s confirmed as administrator.
item.dc:creator: Scott Dance
item.media:credit: Andrew Harnik/Getty Images
item.media:description: Just over a year after being fired, Cameron Hamilton is on the brink of assuming the helm of FEMA once again.

item.title: What Happened When Meta Used A.I. to Ban Accounts on Facebook and Instagram
item.description: Users said the technology mistakenly deleted their accounts. To resolve the issue, they still had to rely on A.I.
item.dc:creator: Stuart A. Thompson and Eli Tan
item.media:credit: Daniel Rodrigues for The New York Times

item.title: Google Releases Three New Gemini A.I. Models
item.description: The models include one that is the company’s most powerful and another that is fine-tuned for cybersecurity, as Google competes with rivals like OpenAI and Anthropic.
item.dc:creator: Kate Conger
item.media:credit: Kelsey McClellan for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Google campus in Mountain View, Calif.

item.title: How Republicans Made Olive Garden Into a Voter ID Talking Point
item.description: Republicans claim that Olive Garden polices its Never-Ending Pasta Bowl more tightly than the United States does its elections. It’s not that simple.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Gabby Jones for The New York Times
item.media:description: An Olive Garden in Manhattan. The restaurant chain has recently found itself at the center of a bitter political debate.

item.title: D.M.V. Error Led to 400 Noncitizens Voting in New Jersey, Governor Says
item.description: The New Jersey Department of Motor Vehicles software glitch also registered roughly 6,600 noncitizens to vote, Gov. Mikie Sherrill announced on Tuesday.
item.dc:creator: Tracey Tully
item.media:credit: Eduardo Munoz/Reuters
item.media:description: People stand at voting booths during a recent election in New Jersey.

item.title: Democrats’ Top Senate Candidate in Maine Won’t Support Schumer for Leader
item.description: Troy Jackson, who is set to become the party’s nominee, also said in an interview that he was not sure he wanted the support of Senator Chuck Schumer in the general election.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: Amanda Sabga/Reuters
item.media:description: Troy Jackson, a former president of the Maine Senate, has little time or money to build a campaign after Graham Platner’s unexpected exit from the Senate race.

item.title: Why Does This Jacket Cost $770? Because It Made Me a New Man.
item.description: Lots of men are geeking out over very expensive clothes — and I became one of them.
item.dc:creator: Sam Anderson

item.title: France Is Poised to Restrict Social Media for Children
item.description: Its new law, which would be the first of its kind enacted by a European country, would ban access to some accounts for anyone under 15.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.media:credit: Manuel Ausloos/Reuters
item.media:description: Using cellphones in Paris this year. Under the terms of the new French law, it would be up to the social media platforms to use age verification systems to filter out underage users.

item.title: A Family’s Daring Escape From North Korea
item.description: Their resolve for freedom hardened during the pandemic, when they lived through a crippling food shortage and were forced to witness frequent public executions.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:credit: Woohae Cho for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kim Yu-mi, right, defected with a group that included her two children and her mother. Life in South Korea has not been quite what they imagined, but they harbor no regrets from deserting the North’s brutal dictatorship and relentless propaganda.

item.title: How a 911 Call Led to the Nation’s Strictest Trans Bathroom Law
item.description: People in Sandpoint, Idaho, don’t know how to characterize a woman’s brush with a transgender woman in a Y.M.C.A. locker room, but the emergency call that followed is still reverberating.
item.dc:creator: Anna Griffin and Loren Elliott For The New York Times

item.title: Cyclospora Spreads Through Human Feces. How Did It Get Into Our Food Supply?
item.description: The large number of cases suggests that excrement entered the food system on a “surprising scale.”
item.dc:creator: Alice Callahan and Caroline Hopkins Legaspi
item.media:credit: Melanie Moser/CDC/Associated Press
item.media:description: These cyclospora oocysts were found in a stool sample. The parasite infects the small intestine, causing frequent watery diarrhea that carries its oocysts, or eggs.

item.title: Why States Are Making It Harder to Save Money With Solar Panels
item.description: The changes are costing homeowners money. But they also reflect a changing grid — and the need for home batteries.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Paris
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Oil Firms Knew for Decades of Methane’s Danger to Planet, Documents Suggest
item.description: The companies knew they were releasing more of the greenhouse gas than they publicly reported, according to industry files cited by the Center for Climate Integrity, an activist organization.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: A gas flare in Eddy County, New Mexico. Methane, a component of natural gas, is a particularly potent greenhouse gas.

item.title: In Venezuela’s Rubble, a Search for the Family Pets
item.description: Volunteers are reuniting pets with families, offering Venezuelans comfort amid loss and devastation.
item.dc:creator: Tibisay Romero and Genevieve Glatsky
item.media:credit: Pedro Mattey/Associated Press
item.media:description: A firefighter rescuing a dog from a building in Caracas that collapsed after twin earthquakes last month.

item.title: ‘Too Rare to Care’? A New Center for Rare Diseases Hopes to Change That
item.description: A new nonprofit wants to streamline gene therapy for diseases often avoided by pharmaceutical companies — making treatment more like a routine procedure than a bespoke drug.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.category: Advanced Research Projects Agency for Health
item.media:credit: Moriah Ratner for The New York Times

item.title: The Best Genetic Risk Tools Don’t Work Equally for Everyone
item.description: Genetic prediction models are poised to revolutionize medicine. But because they have been trained on European DNA, they threaten to widen health care disparities.
item.dc:creator: Emily Baumgaertner Nunn
item.media:credit: Juan Bernabeu

item.title: Restaurant Review: Chez Panisse and Bar Panisse in Berkeley
item.description: The Bay Area farm-to-table classic changed the American restaurant scene. Now it has opened a dynamic new place right next door.
item.dc:creator: Tejal Rao
item.media:credit: Kelsey McClellan for The New York Times

item.title: The Grim Lessons of Israel’s War in Gaza
item.description: Israel has not been held accountable for its actions in Gaza. The nation and the individuals and countries enabling it to escape punishment may only benefit.
item.dc:creator: Omer Bartov
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Trump’s Kiss of Death
item.description: On top of everything else, the president is inept.
item.dc:creator: Thomas B. Edsall
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Photo Illustration by Philotheus Nisch for The New York Times

item.title: Lives Lost
item.description: We look at the American casualties in the war with Iran.
item.dc:creator: Sam Sifton
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: U.S. Army, via Agence France-Presse — Getty Images and Associated Press
item.media:description: Pvt. Isabella Gonzales and First Lt. Tyler James Feehan.

item.title: The Ordeal of a 42-Day Hantavirus Quarantine: ‘I Felt Like a Prisoner’
item.description: Eighteen Americans were isolated for weeks in a Nebraska quarantine facility after being exposed to the deadly virus on a cruise ship. Many found the experience traumatic.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Jordan Gale for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dr. Steve Kornfeld, who was quarantined because of hantavirus exposure, at his home in Bend, Ore., last month.
